objects.name: PAP
objects.description: This defines PAP as a STIX extension

objects.name: ClickFix CastleRAT Operations
objects.description: Velvet Tempest (DEV-0504) is using malvertising and fake CAPTCHAs (ClickFix) to trick users into executing commands that deploy DonutLoader and the CastleRAT backdoor. Though typically a ransomware affiliate, they are focusing on persistent access and environment profiling.

objects.content:
Sector: Non-profit, Cross-sector

Modus operandi:
Initial access via 'ClickFix' social engineering; abuse of Windows utilities (finger.exe, csc.exe) for payload delivery; PowerShell for credential harvesting and persistence.

objects.name: Jasper Sleet AI Personas
objects.description: North Korean actors Jasper Sleet (Storm-0287) and Coral Sleet (Storm-1877) use generative AI to create culturally accurate digital identities, resumes, and communications to infiltrate Western companies as remote IT workers.

objects.content:
Sector: Technology, Software Development

Modus operandi:
Leveraging LLMs to summarize job postings, tailor fake personas, and translate content to maintain high-quality social engineering lures.

objects.name: Dindoor Malware Campaign
objects.description: The Iranian-linked actor MuddyWater is deploying Dindoor malware against U.S. organizations and targeting IP cameras in the Middle East for military reconnaissance.

objects.content:
Sector: Government, Critical Infrastructure, Defense

Modus operandi:
Exploitation of N-day vulnerabilities in Hikvision/Dahua cameras; deployment of custom RATs (Dindoor).

objects.name: Breach: Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI)
objects.description:
Intrusion into an internal unclassified system containing sensitive surveillance data, including 'pen register' and 'trap and trace' records. Attackers exploited commercial ISP infrastructure.

Sector: Government / Law Enforcement

objects.abstract: Geopolitical analysis (EN) / US Cyber Strategy for America (March 2026)
objects.content:
Sector: International Relations / Policy

Theme: US Cyber Strategy for America (March 2026)

The Trump administration's new cyber strategy frames cyberspace as a strategic domain to project power. Key pillars include modernizing federal networks with zero-trust and post-quantum cryptography, and acting proactively to disrupt adversaries.

Tags: Policy, USA, Strategy, Zero-Trust

objects.name: Middle East IP Camera Surveillance Campaign
objects.description:
Surge in scanning and exploitation of Hikvision and Dahua IP cameras across Israel, UAE, Qatar, and Bahrain, attributed to Iranian actors for battle damage assessment.

Analysis:
The campaign highlights the strategic value of IoT in modern warfare, where compromised cameras serve as real-time intelligence nodes.

Recommendations:
- Place IP cameras behind VPN or Zero-Trust gateways and restrict outbound traffic.
- Patch Hikvision products (CVE-2017-7921) and Dahua devices immediately.

objects.content: The cyber threat landscape as of March 8, 2026, is dominated by two major trends: the operationalization of Artificial Intelligence and the intensification of surveillance-focused state-sponsored activity. AI has reached a 'duality' stage; while threat actors like Jasper Sleet (North Korea) use it to create flawless fraudulent identities and automate malware development, defenders like OpenAI and Anthropic are utilizing large-scale AI agents (Codex Security, Claude) to identify thousands of vulnerabilities in critical open-source software and browsers like Firefox. Geopolitically, Middle Eastern tensions have shifted toward large-scale exploitation of IoT infrastructure (IP cameras) for military intelligence and damage assessment. Meanwhile, ransomware affiliates like Velvet Tempest are refining social engineering through the 'ClickFix' technique to bypass traditional perimeters.
